name: Haro: Peñacerrada and Herrera Passes Loop
name_original: Dos puertos míticos: Puerto de Peñacerrada y Puerto de Herrera — circular desde Haro
description: This difficult 37.9-mile road cycling route from Haro tackles the mythical Puerto de Peñacerrada and Puerto de Herrera, gaining 3875 feet.
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Haro traverse the picturesque landscapes of La Rioja Alta, characterized by vast vineyards and fertile plains along the Ebro and Najerilla rivers. The region offers varied terrain, from gentle gradients to challenging ascents into the slender mountains of the Sierra de la Demanda and Sierra de Cantabria. Cyclists can also find routes leading through charming historic villages and past notable landmarks. Best road cycling routes around Haro The most popular road cycling route is Haro: Peñacerrada and Herrera Passes Loop, a 37.9 miles (61.0 km) difficult trail that takes 3 hours 23 minutes to complete. This route features significant elevation gain as it navigates two mountain passes. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Rioja Alta Loop, a moderate 42.5 miles (68.4 km) path. This route offers expansive views of the region's renowned vineyards and takes approximately 3 hours 7 minutes. Local road cyclists also love the Peñacerrada Pass – Zaldiaran Pass (803 m) loop from Haro, a 64.7 miles (104.0 km) trail leading through mountain passes, often completed in about 5 hours 16 minutes. Road cycling around Haro is defined by extensive vineyards, river valleys, and ascents into the Sierra de la Demanda and Sierra de Cantabria. The network offers options for various ability levels, from moderate rides to challenging climbs. The routes in Haro are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 10 reviews. More than 1000 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Haro's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain and scenery can I expect while road cycling around Haro?
A: Road cycling routes around Haro offer a diverse landscape. You'll primarily traverse vast vineyards characteristic of the La Rioja Alta region. Beyond the vineyards, routes follow the fertile plains along the Ebro and Najerilla rivers. For more challenging rides, you can ascend into the slender mountains of the Sierra de la Demanda to the south or the Sierra de Cantabria to the north, which provide stunning panoramic views.

Q: How varied are the road cycling routes around Haro in terms of difficulty?
A: The Haro region caters to a wide range of cycling abilities. You'll find over 60 road cycling routes in total. Approximately 12 routes are considered easy, offering gentler gradients suitable for a relaxed ride. There are 29 moderate routes, providing a good balance of challenge and enjoyment, and 26 difficult routes for experienced cyclists seeking significant elevation gains and demanding climbs.

Q: Are there any historical landmarks or points of interest along the road cycling routes near Haro?
A: Yes, many routes weave through historic villages and past notable landmarks. You can encounter medieval bridges, ancient cave wine presses, and old mills. Specific historical sites include the well-preserved 15th-century &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5343958" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sajazarra Castle&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4705240" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castillo de los Velasco&lt;/a&gt;. The Hermitage of San Felices is another notable stop along certain routes, and the region's famous 'Wine Route' allows you to explore its rich viticultural heritage.

Q: Are there many circular road cycling routes available from Haro?
A: Yes, many of the road cycling routes around Haro are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. Examples include the challenging &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e990696986/haro-penacerrada-and-herrera-passes-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Haro: Peñacerrada and Herrera Passes Loop&lt;/a&gt;, or the longer &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31901014" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peñacerrada Pass – Zaldiaran Pass (803 m) loop from Haro&lt;/a&gt;. These circular routes make planning your ride convenient.

Q: When is the best time of year for road cycling in Haro?
A: The La Rioja Alta region, including Haro, is generally pleasant for cycling for much of the year. Spring and autumn typically offer the most comfortable temperatures and beautiful scenery, with the vineyards in full bloom or displaying their autumnal colors. Summers can be warm, especially in the valleys, while winters are milder but may have cooler temperatures and occasional rain.

Q: Can I find places to stop for refreshments along the road cycling routes?
A: Yes, the region's rich gastronomy and wine culture mean you'll often find charming historic villages and towns along the routes where you can stop for refreshments. Many routes pass through areas with cafes, pubs, and restaurants, allowing you to experience local cuisine and wines, especially along the 'Wine Route'.

Q: Are there any waterfalls accessible or visible from road cycling routes near Haro?
A: While the primary focus of road cycling in Haro is vineyards and mountain passes, some routes may bring you close to areas with natural water features. For dedicated waterfall viewing, you might explore highlights such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5474808" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ollerías Waterfall&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6864843" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Inglares Waterfall&lt;/a&gt;, which are part of broader natural areas around Haro.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about road cycling in Haro?
A: The road cycling experience in Haro is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 10 reviews. Cyclists often praise the stunning vineyard landscapes, the variety of terrain from river valleys to challenging mountain ascents, and the opportunity to explore charming historic villages. Over 1300 road cyclists have used komoot to discover the region's diverse routes.

Q: Are there any easy or family-friendly road cycling options around Haro?
A: Yes, while Haro offers challenging climbs, there are also easier options suitable for less experienced riders or families. Approximately 12 routes are classified as easy, often featuring gentler gradients along river valleys like the Ebro and Najerilla. These routes allow for a more relaxed pace to enjoy the scenic countryside without significant climbs.

Q: What are some of the more challenging road cycling routes in the Haro area?
A: For experienced cyclists seeking a challenge, Haro offers routes with significant elevation gain. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e990696986/haro-penacerrada-and-herrera-passes-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Haro: Peñacerrada and Herrera Passes Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 61 km route that navigates two mountain passes. Another demanding option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/10473372" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peñacerrada Pass – Peñacerrada loop from Haro&lt;/a&gt;, covering 86.9 km with over 1400 meters of ascent.

Q: Can I find parking easily when starting a road cycling route in Haro?
A: Haro is a well-established town, and parking is generally available, especially in and around the town center or designated parking areas. For specific route starting points, it's advisable to check local parking regulations or look for public parking facilities that accommodate vehicles for the duration of your ride.

Q: Are there any specific wine-related attractions I can visit while cycling?
A: Absolutely. The entire region is famous for its winemaking. Many routes are part of or intersect with the 'Wine Route,' allowing you to cycle through extensive vineyards and past numerous wineries. You'll also encounter historic villages known for their winemaking traditions, offering opportunities to learn about and taste local wines.
tips: Mountain pass that joins the provinces of Vitoria and Logroño, between the towns of Peñacerrada and Laguardia, in the middle of what is called the Sierra de Cantabria.
Alava mountains. Road from Lagran with good climbs, shade and good asphalt.
Spectacular views over all of La Rioja Alta.

Thanks to its location you can enjoy both the views of the town and surroundings as well as the parish itself. It is located on an elevation of the land with respect to the town of San Vicente de la Sonsierra, and where it can be seen from many angles around.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this road cycling route?
faqs.answer: This challenging route is designed for experienced riders and typically takes around 3 hours and 20 minutes of active cycling time to complete. However, factoring in stops for views and rest, you should plan for a longer overall duration.

faqs.question: Where does this circular route start, and is there parking available?
faqs.answer: The route starts from Haro. As it's a circular route, you can begin and end in Haro. Parking is generally available in Haro, a well-known town in La Rioja, though specific cycling-dedicated parking areas might require local inquiry upon arrival.

faqs.question: Is this road cycling route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: No, this route is classified as 'difficult' and requires 'very good fitness'. With over 1,180 meters of elevation gain across two significant mountain passes, it is best suited for experienced road cyclists looking for a substantial challenge.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on the Puerto de Peñacerrada and Puerto de Herrera climbs?
faqs.answer: The terrain is generally well-paved and suitable for road cycling, especially on the Puerto de Herrera where the surface is reported to be in very good condition with minimal traffic. However, riders should be prepared for some segments that might be unpaved and difficult to navigate. The climbs feature steep gradients over relatively short distances, characteristic of the Basque Country.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery can I expect along the way?
faqs.answer: You'll experience varied and picturesque landscapes. The lower slopes of the Puerto de Herrera ascent offer sweeping views of a vibrant green Basque valley. Higher up, the route becomes more forested. The region around Haro and the passes is characteristic of the Rioja Alavesa, a renowned wine-producing area, so expect beautiful vineyard landscapes.

faqs.question: Are there any notable viewpoints or landmarks on the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route is named for its two 'mythical ports': Peñacerrada Pass and Puerto de Herrera, both offering significant challenges and views. You'll also pass by Peñacerrada and can enjoy a View of La Rioja. The Church of Santa María la Mayor (San Vicente de la Sonsierra) is another highlight near the route.

faqs.question: Does this trail intersect with other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route briefly intersects with several other paths. Notably, it covers sections of the Camino natural de Ebro - Tramo central, [CIMA LR12] Rivas de Tereso * Labastida, and [CIMA AL05] Herrera, among others.

faqs.question: Is this road cycling route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: As a demanding road cycling route, it is generally not suitable for bringing dogs. The focus is on speed and endurance on paved roads, which isn't an ideal environment for pets.

faqs.question: Do I need any special permits or pay entrance fees to cycle in the Sierra de Cantabria area?
faqs.answer: Generally, no special permits or entrance fees are required for road cycling on public roads through the Sierra de Cantabria. However, always respect local regulations, especially if you venture off paved roads or into protected natural areas.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The best time to cycle this route is typically during the spring (April-June) and autumn (September-October) months. During these periods, the weather is usually milder, offering pleasant temperatures for challenging climbs and avoiding the intense heat of summer or the colder, potentially snowy conditions of winter in the mountain passes.

faqs.question: Are there any cycling-friendly cafes or places to refuel near the route?
faqs.answer: Haro, being the starting and end point, offers various cafes, restaurants, and accommodations. While the route itself is quite remote, you'll find options in Haro to refuel before and after your ride. For specific cycling-friendly establishments, it's best to check local listings or ask once you arrive in Haro.
